design_philosophy.core_loop: Aim light gun at CRT screen and fire at moving targets (ducks or clay pigeons) within three attempts per round, accumulating points and advancing through progressively harder rounds
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Time pressure and accuracy under escalating speed and target complexity; player must hit minimum threshold to advance or face game over
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Arcade-style hunting simulation using novel light gun peripheral; cabinet-like shooting gallery experience brought home
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Hardware-dependent design requiring CRT television and NES Zapper; treats light gun as primary input modality rather than optional control scheme. Simple three-attempt economy creates high stakes per round. Intentionally made dog impossible to shoot on console to enforce family-friendly boundaries (versus arcade version where dog is hittable). Three game modes (A/B/C) with different target types suggest multiple difficulty/playstyle philosophies rather than unified progression

difficulty.lives_system: Three attempts per round to hit targets; failure to meet minimum hit threshold results in game over
difficulty.checkpointing: Round-based progression; completing each round advances difficulty. Round 99 leads to Round 0 (kill screen) with erratic behavior that effectively ends meaningful progression
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player selects from three game modes (A: single duck, B: dual ducks, C: clay pigeons) offering different difficulty curves. Game A allows second player to control duck movement, adding social difficulty negotiation

progression.unlock_mechanism: Progression tied to hit accuracy threshold per round; must shoot minimum number of targets to advance. Difficulty increases via target speed and required hit count. Round structure is sequential and automatic
replayability_hooks: Three distinct game modes with different target types and behaviors
Two-player competitive option in Game A (controller-based duck control)
Score tracking per session encourages high-score chasing
Arcade version bonus stages with dog distraction mechanic adds risk-reward scoring element

world_building_techniques: Hunting theme implicit in visual presentation (ducks, clay pigeons, dog character)
Arcade version dog character with scolding audio feedback suggests personality/narrative presence despite no explicit story

danger_signals: Dog character deliberately made unshootable on console (family-friendly constraint) versus arcade version where it is shootable—signals Nintendo's explicit value boundary around content accessibility
environmental_storytelling: Dog as supporting character in bonus stages (arcade version) grounds action in hunting scenario rather than abstract shooting gallery
